methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 2 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
NuScale Power is a public-stage nuclear energy company that has raised over $600M. The comparable universe in nuclear energy comprises 11 peers total: three public companies (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and eight private firms including Oklo, Nano Nuclear Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power. This sector remains nascent with limited public trading history and heterogeneous technology approaches.

The peer-median triangulation method is not applicable to NuScale's valuation. The nuclear energy peer set contains only two same-stage comparables and zero adjacent-stage or similar-valuation-tier matches, falling short of the three-datapoint minimum required for reliable median analysis. While eight same-sector peers exist, filtering by development stage, regulatory proximity, and capital-intensity yields insufficient overlap. Applying an all-sector median would introduce unacceptable noise given the capital requirements and regulatory timelines unique to nuclear development.

Without viable comparable company triangulation, alternative methods such as discounted cash flow analysis or precedent transaction analysis would be required to establish a defensible valuation range. The key uncertainties limiting peer-based approaches include the early, pre-revenue stage of most nuclear technology developers; regulatory approval dependencies that create discrete valuation inflection points; and significant variation in reactor design, deployment timeline, and addressable market scope across the peer set. Any valuation synthesis would require explicit assumptions about licensing risk, construction timeline, and power purchase agreement pricing to bridge these methodological gaps.
